serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of stir-fried sambal deer meat is approximately 150 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from 'Stir-fried chicken with ginger and spring onion' and 'Deer, cooked, roasted'
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient profile adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo's 'Stir-fried chicken with ginger and spring onion' (ID: 01002) for general stir-fry base, with protein content adjusted upwards based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Deer, cooked, roasted' (FDC ID: 173406) and fat/sodium adjusted for typical sambal preparation.

gi_source.title: Estimated from analogous stir-fried meat dishes with non-starchy vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated based on similar stir-fried meat dishes with low carbohydrate sauces and non-starchy vegetables, as deer meat itself has negligible GI and sambal typically contains minimal glycemic carbohydrates. Value set to 35, categorized as low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sambal paste and seasonings.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation due to its moderate sodium content, and consider requesting reduced salt versions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content, making it a suitable option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and sodium content.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While deer meat is lean, the dish's moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and sodium from sambal warrant moderate consumption for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir Fried Sambal Deer Meat is a flavorful and spicy Chinese-Singaporean dish featuring tender venison (deer meat) stir-fried with a vibrant sambal paste. Sambal, a popular chili-based condiment in Southeast Asia, typically includes a blend of chilies, shallots, garlic, and often shrimp paste, providing a complex spicy, savory, and slightly tangy flavor profile. The deer meat is usually thinly sliced and quickly cooked to retain its tenderness, absorbing the rich flavors of the sambal. This dish is commonly served with steamed rice and can be found in Chinese restaurants, particularly in Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, it offers a good source of lean protein from the deer meat, with moderate fat content from the stir-frying oil and sambal. It is relatively low in carbohydrates and has a low glycemic index, but its sodium content can be moderate to high due to the seasonings and sambal paste, which should be considered by individuals managing blood pressure.

confidence_reason: Nutrient and glycemic index values are estimated based on analogous stir-fried meat dishes from approved sources (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) and typical sambal ingredients, as a direct match for 'Stir Fried Sambal Deer Meat' was not available.
notes: The food group was corrected from 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives' to accurately reflect the primary component. Cuisine tags 'Seafood Dish', 'Crab', and 'Lobster' were removed as they are inconsistent with deer meat. Halal/Kosher status is assumed false due to general preparation methods and lack of certification, though deer meat itself can be halal if slaughtered correctly. Many micronutrient values are set to -1 as they were not available in the primary sources used for estimation and were not part of the core estimation.
